doc.title: Induced conversion — the sweetener
doc.event: The company WANTS its convertible debt gone, so it offers bondholders an extra $15,000 cash to convert now. The conversion itself follows the normal book value method — but the sweetener is an expense of the period, full stop.

lines.label: the extra consideration
lines.why: Anything paid BEYOND the original conversion terms is a cost of getting the debt off the books early — expensed immediately.
lines.trap: Not a reduction of the equity recorded, not a loss on retirement, not PIC — a plain income-statement expense.

lines.label: the sweetener
lines.why: Cash (or the fair value of whatever extra was given).
lines.trap: If the sweetener is extra SHARES instead of cash, expense their fair value — same principle.

entries.caption: Then the ordinary book-value conversion (CV 488,000 → 40,000 shares, $5 par)

lines.label: face
lines.why: The conversion entry is IDENTICAL to an uninduced one — the sweetener never contaminates it.
lines.trap: Blending the 15,000 into this entry is the exam's favorite wrong answer.

lines.label: unamortized remainder
lines.trap: Current as of the conversion date.
Original conversion terms govern the share count.

lines.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par — Common
lines.label: the plug
lines.why: Carrying value minus par capital, as always.

computationPaths.narration: Two separate thoughts: (1) sweetener = expense at fair value; (2) conversion = plain book value method. Keeping them in separate entries is the whole lesson.

memorize.body: Induced conversion = ordinary book-value conversion + Debt Conversion Expense for the fair value of ALL extra consideration (cash, extra shares, sweetened terms).
The sweetener never touches the conversion equity and is never a 'loss on retirement' — it is a current-period expense with its own entry.

questions.prompt: A company pays bondholders $15,000 extra to convert. The $15,000 is recorded as…

distractors.feedback: Not zero, and not buried in PIC — inducements are expensed. Debt Conversion Expense 15,000, its own entry, on the income statement this period.

accountBank.account: Paid-in Capital in Excess of Par — Common

build.scaffold: Two entries, two ideas: pay-and-expense the sweetener first, then run the untouched book-value conversion. If your conversion entry changed because of the inducement, un-blend them.
